Your practice pairs them into a sandwich: premortem before anything with stakes, AAR after everything with learning. Before-action writes the risks; after-action grades which ones showed up and what new volumes the library needs.
chapters.key_takeaway: Intuition is a library and reviews write its volumes — premortem before the stakes, AAR after, and your gut becomes an instrument.

figure.name: Gary Klein
figure.caption: Found that expert intuition is a pattern library built from processed experience — and invented the premortem to write entries in advance.

chapters.title: The founder's four lenses
chapters.body:
The Army reviews maneuvers; you review conversations where money and authority move. So your AARs get four standing lenses — the ones this module's spec names, each targeting a failure mode that quietly costs founders deals. Run them inside question three ('why the gap?') on every rep.

Lens one: clarity under pressure. Find the moments stakes spiked — the price question, the pushback, the 'why should we trust a young company?' Replay your actual sentences. Did structure survive contact (C2's short lines and landings), or did you hedge, qualify, and trail off? Pressure is where your real pitch lives; the calm parts were never the test.

Lens two: naming the ask. Did you actually say the number, the deadline, the next step — explicitly, out loud? The most common founder failure isn't rejection; it's calls that end warmly with no ask ever voiced, both sides feeling good about nothing. Grade every rep binary: the ask was named, or it wasn't. No partial credit for 'they knew what I meant.'

Lens three: where control got handed away. Somewhere in most losing calls there's a hinge — you let them set the follow-up ('we'll get back to you'), accepted a vague objection without a question, started discounting against silence. Find the hinge. It's usually one sentence, and next call's adjustment is what you'll say instead.

Lens four: silence tolerance. Count — from the recording when you have one — how often you broke a silence that the other side should have carried. After the price, after the ask, after their objection restated back to them: whose nerve failed first? The vocal-mechanics module (C2) trained the two-second pause as a skill; this lens audits whether it survives live fire.

Now the automation, because a review system that depends on daily willpower dies by Thursday. Omura already pulls your real interactions — leads, communications, logged calls — into leadership reps with AI after-action reviews attached, scored on exactly these dimensions. Your job in this module is to close the loop the machine can't: add your reflection to each rep — the four questions in your own words, one adjustment named — and carry that adjustment visibly into the next call. The AI drafts the mirror; only you can decide what changes.
chapters.key_takeaway: Clarity under pressure, the ask named, the control hinge, silence tolerance — four lenses on every rep, one adjustment out, machine-assisted but founder-decided.

chapters.title: Automatic means automatic
chapters.body:
The module's title makes a promise most training never keeps: 'becomes automatic.' Here's the honest mechanics of getting there, borrowed from the habit science your Track D runs on.

Automaticity needs a stable cue, and the cue is already in your life: the call ends. That moment — headset off, adrenaline still up — is the trigger. The routine attached to it must be small enough to survive real weeks: ninety seconds, voice or text, four questions, one adjustment. Not the full deep review — that's the weekly session's job — just the hot capture that preserves the raw material. If a rep matters more, flag it for the weekly session where you'll run the full four-lens treatment on the two or three flagged reps, with recordings where they exist. The daily version protects coverage; the weekly version supplies depth. Both together cost you under an hour a week for a compounding asset.

Expect the predictable failure modes and pre-empt them. Week one enthusiasm, week three abandonment: the countermeasure is the size discipline — when busy, shrink the review, never skip it; a two-line AAR keeps the cue alive. The harsh-judge spiral: reviews that become self-punishment get avoided, so enforce the Army norm — causes, not culprits, and every review ends on the adjustment, not the verdict. The theater trap: reviews written and never consulted — beaten by the carry rule: read the last adjustment before the next call. One glance. That single habit closes the loop that makes any of this worth doing.

Definition of done, cold: ten-plus reps logged with your own reflections, not just AI drafts; each has an explicit adjustment; at least three adjustments visibly carried into subsequent calls and re-graded; one premortem run before something that scared you; and — the automaticity test — a week where the post-call capture happened every time without your calendar reminding you. When the hang-up itself asks the four questions, this module is finished and the machine is yours for life. C4 builds on it: the reps compound into relationships, and relationships are the next module's arena.
chapters.key_takeaway: Ninety-second capture on every call-end cue, weekly depth on flagged reps, and read the last adjustment before the next call — that loop is the module.

exercises.task: Run a full four-question AAR on your most recent significant call, applying all four founder lenses in writing.
Open your reps in Omura, pick two AI-reviewed ones, and add your own reflection plus one testable adjustment to each.
Run a premortem on your next scary commitment: assume it failed, write six specific reasons, fix the two cheapest today.
Install the capture habit: define your 90-second post-call routine and run it on every call for the next three days.

quiz.question: Why can twenty years of experience equal one year repeated twenty times?
options: Skills plateau once basic competence is reached
Unreviewed repetition grinds in existing patterns, errors included
Long careers accumulate more atypical cases
Feedback becomes less available with seniority
quiz.objective: Recall why raw experience teaches weakly

quiz.question: The four AAR questions, in order, are…
options: Who failed, why, what punishment, what next
What was intended, what happened, why the gap, what changes
What went well, what went badly, who's responsible, when to retry

quiz.question: Sullenberger's 'bank of experience' line means the Hudson landing was…
options: A withdrawal funded by decades of reviewed training deposits

quiz.question: The premortem works because prospective hindsight…
options: Generates more specific failure causes when the failure is framed as certain

quiz.question: The 'control hinge' lens hunts for…
options: The single moment you handed the conversation's steering away

quiz.question: The 'carry rule' that stops reviews being theater is…
options: Grade each review against the previous one
Schedule reviews at fixed weekly times
Read the last adjustment before the next call
Keep adjustments private until they've worked

_quiz_key.explanation: Without structured feedback, reps reinforce whatever you already do — including the errors.
Intent, actual, causes, adjustment — the NTC's four-question skeleton.
His own framing: small regular deposits, one large withdrawal.
Certainty flips the brain from defending the plan to explaining the corpse.
Usually one sentence — and next call's adjustment replaces it.
One glance at the last adjustment closes the learn-apply loop.

C3.explain_back_prompt: Explain how the AAR converts paid-for reps into compounding skill — the four questions, why immediacy and 'causes not culprits' matter, and your own capture routine.
C3.citation: U.S. Army AAR doctrine (National Training Center); aviation CRM/debrief culture; Klein (recognition-primed decisions, premortem); feedback-loop research
C3.confidence_note: STRONG (mechanism): structured after-action review on real reps is a reliable improvement loop.

C4.big_picture: Winning a room for an hour and keeping people's trust for a decade are different skills wearing the same word — 'influence.' The evidence is honest here: the leadership research is mostly correlational, but its conceptual distinction is rock solid, and every durable fortune runs on the second skill. This module teaches influence that survives months and bad quarters: consistency systems, individualized attention, and public error-owning — measured not by how the pitch landed but by who's still with you next year.

chapters.title: The pitch high and the decade game
chapters.body:
There is a feeling every founder chases: the room leaning in, the jokes landing, the client nodding faster — the pitch high. It's real, it matters, and Track C's earlier modules trained you to produce it on purpose. But watch what happens to businesses built only on that feeling. The charismatic founder closes brilliantly, delivers unevenly, goes quiet between invoices, and resurfaces with the same charm when renewal season comes — and is genuinely confused when the client, who loved the pitch, doesn't renew. The charm was real. It just wasn't the product being bought.

Here is the conceptual distinction this module is built on, and it's the strongest thing in it: single-room charisma and multi-year followership are different constructs. One is a performance skill — energy, structure, presence, deployed in minutes. The other is a custody skill — what you do with someone's trust across hundreds of small, unglamorous interactions when no room is watching: the follow-up sent when promised, the bad news delivered early, the detail about their business remembered in March that they mentioned in November. The research on long-horizon leadership — for all its measurement problems, which the next chapter faces honestly — consistently finds that what followers stay for looks much more like the second list than the first.

Why does this deserve a module of its own in your curriculum? Because your economics already decided it. IronLogic's model — retainers, referrals, small-market reputation — is a decade game wearing monthly invoices. Kingston's business community is a small graph: every client you keep talks to three you haven't met, and every relationship you fumble does too. Gotham needs users to trust it with money decisions across market cycles, which is custody by definition. You are structurally in the multi-year business whether you play it or not.

The good news: custody skills are systems, not gifts. Charisma may have a genetic lottery in it; showing up consistently for eighteen months does not. This module hands you the systems.
chapters.key_takeaway: Charisma is a performance skill; durable influence is a custody skill — and your business model already chose the decade game.

chapters.title: What the research honestly shows
chapters.body:
The academic home of durable influence is transformational leadership — a framework studied across thousands of teams for four decades, and this module's evidence base, so let's handle it with Track B gloves: what it says, and how much to trust it.

The framework identifies four behaviors that distinguish leaders whose people perform beyond contract. Idealized influence: the leader is trusted as consistent and principled — followers report they'd take risks for them because the leader visibly absorbs risk too. Inspirational motivation: a meaningful direction, articulated so the work feels like it's going somewhere. Intellectual stimulation: followers' thinking is challenged and their ideas actually used — the opposite of compliance culture. And individualized consideration: each person treated as a particular human with particular goals, not a headcount. Meta-analyses across military units, hospitals, and firms — Judge and Piccolo's 2004 synthesis is the standard reference — find these behaviors correlate moderately and consistently with follower commitment, satisfaction, and performance: a real, repeated pattern, sized honestly as moderate.

Now the honesty this module's MODERATE tag demands. Most of this evidence is correlational: it can't cleanly separate 'transformational behavior causes commitment' from 'committed teams describe their leaders warmly' or 'thriving organizations afford their leaders the luxury of these behaviors.' Ratings usually come from followers, importing halo effects. And the construct has real academic critics on definitional grounds. So the calibrated read: treat the four behaviors as a well-evidenced description of what durable followership looks like around leaders who have it, and a plausible-but-unproven recipe for producing it.

Here's why the bet is still excellent, in Track B terms: the behaviors are cheap, reversible, and independently valuable. Remembering clients' contexts, keeping promises visibly, using people's ideas, explaining where the work is going — these cost discipline, not capital, and they're defensible business practice even if the causal arrow turns out weaker than hoped. Asymmetric bet, honest label. That's how this curriculum buys ideas.
chapters.key_takeaway: Four behaviors — trusted consistency, meaningful direction, ideas used, individuals seen — well-evidenced as description, plausible as recipe, cheap either way.

diagram.title: Reading correlational evidence like Track B

chapters.title: The letter writer
chapters.body:
Warren Buffett has run Berkshire Hathaway since 1965, and the mechanism of his influence is so unfashionable it's almost invisible: he writes a letter. Every year, the same voice — plain, funny, allergic to jargon — explains to shareholders what happened, what he got right, and, famously, what he got wrong. The mistakes section is the load-bearing wall: he has called buying the original Berkshire textile business itself his biggest mistake — once quantifying it, in a 2010 interview, as a roughly two-hundred-billion-dollar error in forgone compounding — mocked his own airline investments, and named errors of omission no one would have caught. Sixty years of that voice built something no marketing budget can buy: an audience of owners who held through crashes because the person holding their money had never once managed their perception.

Look at the machinery underneath, because it maps one-to-one onto this module's framework. Trusted consistency: the letters never change voice in good years or bad — no euphemisms in 2008, no victory laps in bull markets. That sameness IS the product; people can extend trust across time only to something that behaves predictably across time. Owning errors publicly: each admitted mistake spends a little short-term image and buys long-term credibility at compound rates — because an audience that has watched you volunteer your failures believes your successes without an audit. And direction: the letters teach — float, moats, incentives — so shareholders aren't just informed but upgraded, which is intellectual stimulation performed on a few hundred thousand people at once.

The same custody shows in how he buys companies: family sellers have accepted his bid over higher ones because his handshake reputation — managers left alone, promises kept for decades — was worth more than the spread. Reputation literally prices in his market.

Your scale version is embarrassingly available: a short, regular, personally-voiced letter — monthly to IronLogic clients, quarterly to Gotham users — that reports honestly, includes what went wrong, and teaches something. Nobody in your market does this. Sixty years is a long compounding runway, and you're starting at eighteen.
chapters.key_takeaway: The same honest voice in good years and bad, mistakes volunteered first — trust compounds on predictability, and a regular letter is its cheapest instrument.

figure.name: Warren Buffett
figure.caption: Sixty years of plain-voiced letters that volunteer his own mistakes — consistency and confessed error compounding into unbuyable trust.

chapters.title: Sixteen years of boring
chapters.body:
Angela Merkel led Germany for sixteen years — four consecutive terms through the financial crisis, the euro crisis, and a pandemic — and she did it with a style that violates every charisma manual ever sold. Flat delivery. No soaring speeches. A wardrobe chosen to end wardrobe discussions. Her political brand became a verb — 'merkeln,' to wait and say little — coined as mockery and gradually repriced as description of a method. The method: a physicist's habit of gathering evidence before moving, positions changed rarely and explained when changed, and a personal steadiness so unvarying that a large, anxious electorate used it as an anchor. Germans called her 'Mutti' — Mum — not because she inspired them but because they could predict her, and in turbulent decades predictability outbid inspiration at the ballot box four times running. (Honor the counter-case too, because low variance has a failure mode: the same steadiness read, to critics of her refugee-policy years and energy bets, as slowness and rigidity — predictability compounds trust only while the underlying calls stay sound. C5's review machinery exists precisely because consistency without inspection calcifies.)

The founder lesson is precise, so cut it carefully. This is not an argument that dullness wins or that you should suppress the energy that closes your pitches — C1 and C2 exist because performance matters. It's evidence about which variable carries the long game: variance. Merkel's genius was low personal variance — the same person in crisis meetings as in press conferences, this year as last year. Long-horizon trust is, mathematically, a prediction problem: people commit multi-year resources — votes, retainers, capital — to what they can forecast. Every mood swing, every overpromise-underdeliver cycle, every reinvention widens your error bars, and wide error bars price you out of decade-length commitments no matter how brilliant your peaks.

There's a Gotham-specific edge in this chapter too: financial products live or die on exactly this variable. Users forgive modest returns; they never forgive being surprised by their money app. Honest screens, stable language, no silent changes — product-shaped low variance.

So the practice: pick the three or four behaviors you want people to bank on — response time, honest bad news, the monthly letter, prices that mean what they say — and make them boring. Ruthlessly, deliberately boring. Save the fireworks for the demo; the retainer is signed with the metronome.
chapters.key_takeaway: Long-horizon trust is a prediction problem: pick the behaviors people bank on and make them metronome-boring. Peaks close pitches; low variance signs retainers.

figure.name: Angela Merkel
figure.caption: Four terms won on low personal variance — 'merkeln' began as mockery and got repriced as method. Predictability outbid inspiration.

chapters.title: The trust ledger
chapters.body:
Systems now, because custody without bookkeeping is sentiment. Three instruments, all cheap, all running on infrastructure you already have.

Instrument one: the promise log. Every commitment you voice — 'I'll send that Thursday,' 'we'll have it live by month-end,' 'I'll introduce you to her' — gets captured the moment it's made (Omura's tasks exist for exactly this) and gets one of two outcomes: kept visibly, or renegotiated early. The renegotiation rule is the professional secret: trust isn't damaged by the deadline you moved on Tuesday for Friday's delivery; it's damaged by the silence that made them ask. Early renegotiation is a kept promise about the promise. Your metric, reviewed monthly: promises kept-or-renegotiated as a percentage of promises made. That number is your durable-influence Brier score.

Instrument two: the relationship file. For each client and key contact, a living note: their stated goals, their constraints, the names they mention (the partner, the kid doing exams, the dead server that ruined their March), what they care about beyond the contract. Review it before every call. This is individualized consideration made mechanical — and the mechanics matter, because 'caring about people' as a personality trait is unreliable at scale, while caring as a pre-call checklist survives busy quarters. When you open with 'did the CXC results come out okay?' three months after they mentioned it once, you are doing what the research says durable leaders do — seeing the particular person — with a system doing the remembering.

Instrument three: the letter, from the Buffett chapter — monthly, short, honest, one taught thing, one owned miss when there is one. It bundles all four research behaviors into a single repeatable artifact and doubles as your consistency metronome: it ships on the same day every month or it isn't a metronome.

The module's culminating artifact assembles itself from these: one real client relationship sustained six-plus months, with the ledger showing what sustained it — promises kept, contexts remembered, letters shipped, at least one error owned early. Not a feeling of good relationship: receipts.
chapters.key_takeaway: Promise log with early renegotiation, pre-call relationship files, and the monthly letter — custody made mechanical, with receipts.

diagram.title: The custody loop, monthly
nodes: Ship the letter, same day monthly
Audit the ledger — receipts, not vibes

chapters.title: Becoming someone people bank on
chapters.body:
Zoom out to what this module changes about your trajectory, because durable influence is the quietest compounding asset in the whole curriculum — and the one your age makes most valuable.

Run the arithmetic of the small market. Kingston's serious business community is a few thousand people who all eventually talk. A founder who burns bright and delivers unevenly generates mixed references, and mixed references cap growth invisibly — you never see the deals that didn't call. A founder whose ledger runs clean for five years becomes something markets price like AAA credit: the safe choice that procurement can defend, the name that comes up when someone asks 'who actually delivers?' You cannot buy that positioning with marketing, and — this is the strategic gift of starting at eighteen — you can be the twenty-three-year-old with a five-year clean ledger, an asset combination that essentially doesn't exist in your market. Every year early is a year of compounding your competitors can never buy back.

Notice also how this module locks into the others. The C3 review loop is what keeps delivery quality worthy of the trust the ledger tracks — custody of trust without competence is just a longer con. C1's prestige economics are the same physics at relationship timescale: freely conferred status, maintained by demonstrated value, decaying only if you stop earning it. And B1's calibration shows up here in miniature: the promise log is literally a forecast log — every 'Thursday' is a prediction, and keeping your word is being calibrated about yourself.

Definition of done, cold: the promise log live for a month with its percentage computed; relationship files on your top five contacts, actually consulted pre-call; two letters shipped on schedule; one error owned early and in writing; and the six-month relationship artifact documented with its receipts. The becoming, in one sentence: stop trying to be impressive and start being bankable — impressive gets you meetings; bankable gets you a decade.
chapters.key_takeaway: A clean five-year ledger at twenty-three is an asset your market has never seen — impressive gets meetings; bankable gets the decade.

exercises.task: Start the promise log: capture every commitment you voice for one week, then compute kept-or-renegotiated percentage.
Build relationship files for your top 5 contacts: goals, constraints, mentioned names, what they care about beyond the contract.
Draft and send your first monthly letter: what happened, one thing taught, one miss owned if there is one.
Find one promise currently at risk and renegotiate it early — today, before they have to ask.

C4.explain_back_prompt: Explain why single-pitch charisma and multi-year followership are different constructs, and which systems convert the second one from sentiment into receipts.
C4.citation: Transformational-leadership meta-analyses (correlational — honesty noted); Berkshire Hathaway shareholder letters (primary source); long-horizon trust practice
C4.confidence_note: MODERATE (correlational) / STRONG (conceptual distinction): single-room charisma and multi-year followership are different constructs.

C5.big_picture: Anyone can spike one great meeting. The rarest leadership skill — and the slowest to build — is holding a room that recurs: the weekly session people keep attending with their attention, not just their bodies, for a quarter, a year, a decade. This module teaches the mechanics of recurring rooms — ritual, candor, cadence — through the best-documented one in business history, and its checkpoint project has you design and launch your own. This is Track C's capstone because it's where presence becomes institution.

chapters.title: The second meeting problem
chapters.body:
Every skill Track C has trained so far — prestige posture, vocal command, reviewed reps, trust custody — reaches its final exam in a format nobody warns you about: the meeting that happens again. The first session of anything runs on novelty; everyone performs, including you. The fifth session is where truth arrives. Attention frays, the same two people talk, the agenda calcifies into ritual-without-content, and the room's honest verdict shows up as multitasking and 'sorry, can we push this week?' A room you hold once is a performance. A room you hold for a quarter is an institution — and institutions are what founders actually need to build, because everything durable in a company lives inside some recurring room: the client status call, the team standup, the review cadence, eventually the board meeting.

Be honest about why this is the slowest-building skill in the track, because the module's quarterly pacing isn't decoration. Single meetings give you C2's fast feedback — you feel the room in real time. Recurring rooms give feedback on a delay of weeks: the cost of a soft session shows up two sessions later as attrition; the payoff of good ritual shows up in month two as people arriving prepared. The evidence base here is the same as C4's — long-horizon leadership research, honestly correlational — plus something sturdier: the documented practice of rooms that visibly worked for decades, which this module mines for mechanics.

And name what's actually being built, so the project has a target. A recurring room that works produces three outputs no single meeting can: accumulated context (the room remembers, so decisions build instead of restart), licensed candor (people learn what's safe to say by watching what happened last time — safety is empirical, never declared), and rhythm (deadlines and reviews stop being events and become weather). Context, candor, cadence. Every mechanic in the chapters ahead serves one of those three.
chapters.key_takeaway: First meetings run on novelty; fifth meetings run on structure. Context, candor, cadence — the three outputs only a recurring room can produce.

columns.label: Meeting one
points: Tells you nothing about the room

columns.label: Meeting five
points: Structure carries it — or nothing does

chapters.title: Inside the Braintrust
chapters.body:
The best-documented recurring room in modern business sits inside Pixar. Ed Catmull, its co-founder and president, considered the Braintrust — the recurring session where directors show unfinished films to a group of peers — the studio's actual competitive advantage, and he wrote the mechanics down in unusual detail. Every Pixar film, he insisted, 'sucks' in its early versions; the Braintrust's job was to make each one un-suck through candor that most organizations can't sustain past one meeting. It ran for decades across dozens of films. Study its rules the way you studied Kelly sizing, because each one is engineering.

Rule one: the room has no authority. The Braintrust gives notes; the director decides. Catmull identified this as the load-bearing wall — the moment feedback becomes command, presenters stop bringing real problems and start managing the room, and the session dies as an information source while continuing to meet. Preserve this in your rooms: advice flows in, authority stays with the owner.

Rule two: notes target the work, never the person. 'The second act loses the character's want' is a note; 'you always overcomplicate' is an attack. The distinction is what lets candor recur — people return to rooms that cut their work sharply and their person not at all.

Rule three: problems, not prescriptions. The Braintrust names what's broken; it doesn't co-direct the fix. This keeps ownership — and therefore effort — with the maker, and keeps the room's ego out of the film.

Rule four: peers, not bosses, and unfinished work only. Showing polished work invites applause; showing broken work invites the only feedback worth convening for. The room's culture must make 'here's where I'm stuck' higher status than 'here's how great this is' — prestige economics, C1, applied to candor. And the honest failure note, from Catmull himself: the Braintrust broke down on some productions — candor decays quietly when hierarchy creeps back in or when a room's members start managing each other's feelings. Even the best-documented recurring room needed its own repair cycles, which is exactly why the quarterly self-review below is a rule and not a suggestion.

Your scale swap is direct: a weekly build-review where you and collaborators show unfinished client work; a client room where the agenda leads with 'what's not working.' The rules port whole.
chapters.key_takeaway: No authority in the room, notes on the work not the person, problems not prescriptions, unfinished work only — candor that can recur.

figure.name: Ed Catmull
figure.caption: Built the Braintrust — decades of recurring candor, held together by one rule: the room advises, the maker decides.

chapters.title: The standard-setter
chapters.body:
Vince Lombardi took over the Green Bay Packers in 1959, a team that had won one game the previous season, and built the most dominant run in NFL history — five championships in seven years — substantially inside one recurring room: the film session and practice meeting, held daily, for years. (Sourcing note, per this curriculum's standards: the Lombardi material comes from player memoirs and David Maraniss's biography — rich secondary sources, not first-person management writing like Catmull's — so treat this chapter as a well-attested illustration rather than documented mechanics.) What his players described afterward, almost uniformly, wasn't tactical genius. It was standards, enforced through repetition until they became identity. 'Lombardi time' — arrive fifteen minutes early or you're late — sounds like a slogan until you understand it as mechanics: a recurring room's opening minutes teach everyone what this room's gravity is, and Lombardi made punctuality itself the first standard everyone rehearsed, every day, before a word was spoken.

His deeper method is the one your project needs: he taught fundamentals in the meeting, repeatedly, to professionals. The famous season-opening line — 'Gentlemen, this is a football' — was delivered to men who had played the game their whole lives, and it wasn't condescension; it was a declaration that this room does not skip steps, and that mastery of basics is never beneath anyone. Recurring rooms drift toward assuming their fundamentals — the client call that stops reviewing the actual numbers, the standup that stops stating the goal. The Lombardi move is ritualized return: the same few load-bearing questions, every session, no exceptions, precisely because they're basic. In your build-review that might be 'what did the client actually see this week?' In the Gotham room, 'what would a user say broke?' Boring on purpose; boring is what makes the exceptional visible against it.

One honest caution, C1 in hand: Lombardi ran hot, and mythology remembers the yelling. Read the record more carefully — players stayed loyal for decades and named the consistency and the care under the volume, not the volume itself, as what held them. Port the standards and the ritualized fundamentals; leave the heat. Your rooms run on prestige economics, and the evidence from your own track says that's the compounding path.
chapters.key_takeaway: Rooms drift from their fundamentals — ritualize the return: same load-bearing questions every session, boring on purpose.

figure.name: Vince Lombardi
figure.caption: Five championships built in a daily recurring room — standards rehearsed until they were identity. 'Gentlemen, this is a football.'

chapters.title: The mechanics of a room that recurs
chapters.body:
Now the assembly manual — six mechanics, each mapped to the outputs from chapter one, each cheap enough to run in a two-person company.

One: fixed slot, fixed length, shorter than comfortable. Cadence is the point — same day, same time, and end early rather than pad. A twenty-five-minute room people trust beats an hour they dread; recurring rooms die of flabbiness before they die of brevity.

Two: an agenda skeleton that never changes, with flesh that always does. Three to five standing sections — last session's commitments checked, the fundamentals question, the open problem, decisions, new commitments — so the room accumulates context instead of restarting. The skeleton is your Lombardi ritual; the open-problem slot is your Braintrust.

Three: the room's memory is written. One shared note per session: decisions, owners, dates. Two minutes at the close. Without written memory the room has amnesia and re-litigates; with it, the 'commitments checked' opener has teeth — and your C4 promise-log discipline becomes communal.

Four: rotate the microphone deliberately. Recurring rooms develop gravity wells — the same voices, usually including yours, expand. Assign the open-problem presentation on rotation; ask the quietest attendee's opinion by name once per session. What the room hears from itself teaches it who it is.

Five: candor rules, stated once and enforced forever. Steal Pixar's four wholesale, and add the enforcement mechanic: the first time someone attacks a person instead of work — including you in your own head — the correction happens in-room, calmly, immediately. Safety is built from witnessed precedent, never from kickoff speeches.

Six: the quarterly AAR on the room itself. Every twelve or thirteen sessions, the meeting reviews the meeting — C3's four questions pointed inward: what was this room supposed to produce, what did it produce, why the gap, what changes? Rooms that never review themselves calcify; the review is how the institution stays alive rather than merely scheduled.
chapters.key_takeaway: Fixed short slot, unchanging skeleton, written memory, rotated voice, enforced candor, quarterly self-review — six mechanics, all cheap.

chapters.title: A quarter of holding it
chapters.body:
The project attached to this module asks for something no other checkpoint has: time. Design the room in a week; the holding takes the quarter, and the quarter is the curriculum. Here's what the three months will actually teach, so you recognize the lessons as they arrive rather than reading them as failure.

Weeks one to three: the novelty subsidy. Sessions run warm, attendance is easy, and you'll be tempted to conclude the room works. Bank nothing yet; log everything — attendance, who spoke, whether commitments closed. Your baseline is being written.

Weeks four to seven: the sag. Novelty expires on schedule. A session gets postponed, the open-problem slot gets a shallow offering, someone — possibly you — arrives unprepared. This is the exam, and the answers are mechanical, not motivational: shrink the meeting rather than skip it (fifteen disciplined minutes preserves the cadence a cancellation breaks — Track D's never-zero rule, applied to a room); run the commitments check without softening it; and when the open problem is thin, present your own thinnest work to re-license vulnerability. The sag is where most recurring rooms quietly die. Surviving it — not brilliantly, just structurally — is the skill this module exists to build.

Weeks eight to thirteen: compounding or theater. By now the room is either accumulating — commitments closing faster, candor arriving without invitation, decisions building on written memory — or it's performing the agenda as ritual theater. The quarterly AAR at the end tells you which, with receipts: the logs you kept, attendance and preparation trends, the ratio of problems-surfaced to problems-decided. Either verdict wins: a compounding room is an institution you now own the blueprint for; a theatrical one, honestly diagnosed, is the most instructive failure available — most founders never learn why their meetings are dead because they never measured one.

Definition of done lives in the project brief below. The becoming, stated once: a founder who can hold a recurring room can eventually hold a company, a board, a market's attention — because they're all the same skill at different radii. This is where the radius starts.
chapters.key_takeaway: Weeks 1-3 are subsidized, weeks 4-7 are the exam, weeks 8-13 reveal compounding or theater — survive the sag structurally, then read the receipts.

exercises.task: Choose your recurring room and write its charter: a one-sentence purpose statement, the attendee list, and one measurable 90-day outcome the room exists to move.
Write the agenda skeleton: 3-5 standing sections including commitments-check, one fundamentals question, and a Braintrust-rules open-problem slot.
Draft the candor rules card (Pixar's four, in your words) you'll state at session one and enforce thereafter.
Set up the room's written memory: the shared note template with decisions, owners, dates — and the session log for attendance and speaking balance.
