project_brief.title: Design and hold a recurring room for a quarter
project_brief.description: Create one real recurring session — client status room, build review, or team standup — and hold it for a full quarter: fixed cadence, unchanging skeleton, Braintrust candor rules, written memory, and session logs. The project submission is the design plus the first month's receipts and the mid-quarter self-review; the quarter completes on your calendar. This is Track C's capstone: presence, reps, and trust custody assembled into an institution.

steps.title: Design the room
steps.detail: Purpose, attendees, fixed slot and length, agenda skeleton, candor rules card, and the written-memory template.

steps.title: Run sessions 1-4 with full logging
steps.detail: Hold the cadence; log attendance, speaking balance, commitments made and closed each session.

steps.title: Survive one thin week structurally
steps.detail: When the sag hits, shrink — never skip; document what you did to hold the cadence.

steps.title: Run the mid-quarter AAR on the room
steps.detail: C3's four questions pointed at the room itself, with the logs as evidence; one structural adjustment adopted.

steps.title: Write the submission
steps.detail: The design, four-plus sessions of receipts, the sag story, the AAR verdict and adjustment — and your quarter-completion plan.

rubric.criterion: Room genuinely held — 4+ real sessions logged
Mechanics implemented: skeleton, candor rules, written memory
Sag survived structurally and documented
Honest mid-quarter AAR with one adopted change

C5.confidence_note: MODERATE: durable leadership evidence is mostly correlational; the structural point is sound.

D1.big_picture: Motivation is a weather system; you're trying to build infrastructure. This module replaces the surge-and-crash cycle with habit architecture — the specific, research-backed conditions under which behaviors become automatic: one stable cue, consistent timing, the smallest viable version, chosen by you, with the good feeling engineered in. The honest timeline: automaticity takes about two months of daily repetition, not twenty-one days. Your artifact is a streak that survives nine real weeks.

chapters.title: Infrastructure beats weather
chapters.body:
You already know the cycle because you've lived it: a surge of motivation — new plan, new discipline, 5am alarms — followed by eight days of execution, then a bad night, a client fire, a missed session, and the whole structure collapses into three weeks of nothing plus a layer of self-contempt that makes restarting harder than starting was. Then another surge. Most ambitious people run this loop for decades and call the surge phase 'being disciplined' and the crash phase 'losing discipline,' as if discipline were a substance that leaks.

The reframe this module is built on: motivation is weather — genuinely variable, partly outside your control, and a terrible foundation for anything important. What reliable people have is not better weather; it's infrastructure — behaviors that run on automatic triggers rather than daily decisions, the way you don't decide to brush your teeth, you just find yourself brushing. The psychology term is automaticity: with enough context-consistent repetition, a behavior migrates from the deliberate, energy-hungry system that debates everything to the cue-driven system that just executes. Once a behavior is automatic, motivation becomes irrelevant to whether it happens — which is the entire point, because you're building companies whose demands will regularly flatten your motivation for weeks at a time.

Notice this module's position in your curriculum: it's load-bearing for everything else. The A1 study engine, B1's weekly forecasts, C3's post-call reviews, the training that Track E frames — every system this curriculum installs is, mechanically, a habit, and every one will die in the motivation crash unless it's built to the architecture this module teaches. That's why the evidence tag here is STRONG and the artifact is unglamorous: one habit, built correctly, surviving nine consecutive weeks including the bad ones. Not five habits for eight days. One, for sixty-three days — because the research says sixty-three days is roughly what the wiring actually costs.
chapters.key_takeaway: Motivation is weather; automaticity is infrastructure — and every other system in this curriculum is a habit that dies without it.

columns.label: Cue-driven (automatic)
points: Bad weeks shrink it, don't kill it

chapters.title: The honest timeline: ~60 days, not 21
chapters.body:
The most repeated number in self-improvement — 'it takes 21 days to form a habit' — is folklore, traceable to a 1960s plastic surgeon's observation about patients adjusting to their new faces, not to any behavioral study. The real research says something slower and more useful. The landmark daily-tracking study — Lally and colleagues, 2010 — followed people repeating a chosen behavior in a consistent context and measured automaticity daily; the median time to plateau was around sixty-six days, with enormous spread (their estimates ran from 18 to over 250 days) — simple habits (drinking water after breakfast) wired in weeks, effortful ones (exercise) took months, and some participants were still climbing at the study's end. In 2024, Singh and colleagues systematically reviewed the accumulated literature — thousands of participants across studies — and landed in the same neighborhood: the median time to automaticity runs roughly fifty-nine to sixty-six days, with health behaviors clustering around two months of consistent repetition.

Three design consequences fall out of the honest number, and they're worth more than the number itself. First, calibrate expectations like a Track B graduate: at week three — exactly where the folklore says you should be done — you are, statistically, a third of the way. The gap between folk expectation and real timeline is where most habit attempts die, because people read normal-speed progress as personal failure. You now can't make that read.

Second, Lally's automaticity curves were negatively accelerated — steepest gains early, flattening later — which means the early days matter disproportionately for wiring and the later days for consolidation. Protect the first two weeks fiercely; coast into automaticity after week six.

Third — also from Lally's daily-tracking data — missing a single day produced no measurable damage to the automaticity curve. One miss is noise. What kills habits is the miss that becomes a break: two days becoming five becoming 'I used to do that.' The rule this yields — never miss twice — is the single most protective behavior in this entire module, and this app's streak system is built around it.
chapters.key_takeaway: Median ~59-66 days to automaticity; week three is one-third done, not failed. One miss is noise — never miss twice.

chapters.title: The five conditions
chapters.body:
The architecture itself — five conditions the module description names, each with its evidence and its failure mode. Design your habit against all five before day one; retrofits are expensive.

One: a stable cue. Automaticity is cue-binding — the behavior attaches to a trigger, not to an intention. Kaushal and Rhodes tracked new gym members for twelve weeks and found consistency of cues, not enthusiasm, predicted who developed exercise habits — with their data suggesting frequent, cue-consistent repetition (on the order of several sessions weekly across roughly the first six weeks) as the pattern that wired. Pick one concrete, unmissable trigger — 'after I pour the morning coffee,' 'when the laptop opens' — never a time-vague 'in the morning.' The cue is the habit's address; a behavior with no fixed address never gets found.

Two: consistent timing, morning if possible. Mornings carry two structural advantages: fewer competing demands have accumulated (the day hasn't stolen you yet), and self-control research suggests the will is freshest early. Your energy-aware daily sessions in this app already lean this way; align the new habit with it.

Three: smallest viable version. The single most violated condition. The habit you're wiring is the showing up, not the volume — two minutes of code review, one forecast question, five minutes of bagwork. Volume is trivially expandable once the trigger fires automatically; but wire a 90-minute monster and every rep needs heroic weather, which is the exact dependency you're eliminating. Small enough to survive your worst realistic day — that's the sizing rule.

Four: self-selected. Habits imposed by others carry a compliance tax; chosen ones borrow identity's momentum. The research on autonomy in behavior change is consistent: you defend what you authored. This curriculum hands you candidates; you pick.

Five: positive affect, engineered in. Reward learning is the engine underneath — behaviors that feel good on completion wire faster. Immediately after the rep, mark it: check the streak, log the win, note the satisfaction. Small, immediate, reliable — that contingency, repeated sixty times, is what 'discipline' is actually made of.
chapters.key_takeaway: Stable cue, morning slot, smallest viable version, chosen by you, immediate good feeling — all five, designed before day one.

chapters.title: Franklin's speckled axe
chapters.body:
In his twenties, Benjamin Franklin — printer, later founder, diplomat, scientist — designed what is probably history's most famous personal-discipline system, and documented it in his autobiography with an honesty that makes it more useful, not less. He listed thirteen virtues — temperance, order, industry, and so on — and ruled a small notebook: virtues down the side, days of the week across the top. Each evening he reviewed the day and marked a black spot for every lapse. Crucially, he didn't attempt all thirteen at once: he gave each virtue a week of primary focus while merely tracking the rest, cycling through the full list four times a year. One priority at a time, everything measured, progress visible on paper — an eighteenth-century implementation of almost everything this module teaches: small focus, stable ritual (the evening review was itself the cue), immediate feedback, self-selected targets.

What the system produced is instructive in both directions. Franklin credited it — decades later — with much of his success and happiness, and the industriousness and order it drilled show all over his output. But he also reported the honest results: 'Order' defeated him more or less permanently (his papers stayed chaotic all his life), the black spots never fully disappeared, and he confessed the project of moral perfection itself was abandoned. Then he told a joke that is secretly the deepest sentence in the autobiography: he compared himself to a man who, buying an axe from a smith, wanted the whole surface polished, tired mid-grinding, and declared 'I think I like a speckled axe best.'

Read the speckled axe correctly and it will save your nine-week artifact. It is not permission to quit — Franklin ran his tracker for years. It's the mature settlement between standards and self-contempt: the system's job is the tracking and the trend, not the perfect page. Your streak will take hits. The founder who expects a speckled axe keeps grinding; the one who demanded mirror-polish throws the axe away at the first spot — which is the motivation crash from chapter one, wearing colonial clothes.
chapters.key_takeaway: Franklin ran the tracker for years and stayed speckled — the system's job is the trend, not the perfect page. Perfectionism is the crash in disguise.

figure.name: Benjamin Franklin
figure.caption: Tracked thirteen virtues in a ruled notebook, one focus per week — history's most famous habit tracker, and honest about his lapses.

chapters.title: The chain, the app, and the never-zero rule
chapters.body:
The modern folklore version of Franklin's notebook is the calendar-and-marker method attributed to Jerry Seinfeld: write jokes every day, mark a red X on the calendar for each day done, and after a week or two your only job is 'don't break the chain.' (Honest sourcing, since this curriculum keeps its receipts: the story entered circulation through a young comedian's account of backstage advice; Seinfeld has since said, with characteristic dryness, that the method isn't really his invention — while the daily-writing discipline itself absolutely was his practice.) Whoever owns the branding, the mechanism is real and maps directly onto this module's science: the chain makes the streak visible (feedback), makes each day's stake concrete (loss aversion — you now have something to lose), and converts a vague aspiration into a binary daily question with a two-minute answer.

Your version of the calendar is this app, and its design encodes the research deliberately. The streak counter is the chain. The daily session is the stable cue slot. And the energy selector — the 'drained' option that shrinks the day's work to a minimal pass — implements the most important rule in habit maintenance: never zero. On your worst day, the smallest viable version still counts, because what you're protecting isn't the volume, it's the cue-behavior bond and the identity that's crystallizing around it. A five-minute drained-day pass preserves both; a zero breaks both and hands the next day to the 'never miss twice' emergency protocol.

Which brings the lapse playbook, written now while you're calm. Miss one day: no story, no penance — the data says it costs nothing; execute tomorrow's rep at the normal size and the chain's spirit survives. Miss two: alarm state — tomorrow's rep happens at the smallest viable size no matter what, and you diagnose the cause structurally, not morally: did the cue disappear (travel, schedule change)? Then the fix is a backup cue, not more willpower. Was the version too big? Shrink it permanently. Structural diagnosis, structural repair — you are the architect here, not the defendant.
chapters.key_takeaway: The chain makes the stake visible; never-zero protects the bond on drained days; misses get structural diagnosis, never moral trials.

figure.name: Jerry Seinfeld
figure.caption: The daily-writing chain method carries his name — mark the X, don't break the chain. (He credits the discipline, not the invention.)

diagram.title: The lapse playbook
nodes: Diagnose the cue or size — fix structure

chapters.title: Nine weeks, one habit
chapters.body:
Now the build, concretely, because this module's artifact is empirical: a streak that survives nine weeks — sixty-three days, sitting deliberately at the far edge of the median automaticity window.

Week zero — design on paper before day one. Choose ONE habit (the others wait; Franklin's one-per-week focus was correct three centuries ago and the modern data agrees — simultaneous habit launches split the repetition budget). Given your curriculum, the highest-leverage candidates: the A1 study block, the C3 post-call capture, or the morning session in this app that bundles several. Write the five conditions explicitly: the exact cue, the time, the smallest viable version (test: doable on your worst realistic day?), why you chose it, and how completion gets marked. Then pre-write the lapse playbook answers: what's the backup cue when the schedule breaks? What does the drained-day version look like?

Weeks one and two — protect the wiring. This is the steep part of the curve and the fragile part of the project. Treat the cue slot as untouchable in your calendar; when conflicts come — and a client will absolutely fire something at you in week two — the habit shrinks to its minimum and still runs. Log daily in the app; the streak number is your feedback loop, and per chapter three's fifth condition, let yourself actually feel the mark.

Weeks three through six — the boring middle. Automaticity is climbing but not arrived; motivation's novelty subsidy has expired (you know this pattern from C5's rooms — same physics). The discipline here is almost entirely about the never-zero rule and refusing the upgrade temptation: do NOT grow the habit yet. Size stability is what lets the trigger bind.

Weeks seven through nine — consolidation and the test. Somewhere in here, notice the module's real finish line: the day you find yourself mid-habit without remembering deciding. That felt shift — behavior arriving unbidden — is automaticity announcing itself. Now, and only now, expand the volume if you want to. Definition of done, cold: sixty-three days, streak intact under never-zero rules, lapse playbook used at least once and documented, and the felt shift noted in writing. Then D2 — where the discipline you've built gets pointed at effort itself.
chapters.key_takeaway: One habit, five conditions written before day one, size frozen until week seven — done is 63 days plus the felt shift, not a perfect page.

exercises.task: Design your one habit on paper: exact cue, time, smallest viable version, why you chose it, completion mark — all five conditions explicit.
Pre-write the lapse playbook: your backup cue for broken schedules and the exact drained-day minimum version.
Run the worst-day test: simulate your most realistic terrible day and verify the minimum version genuinely fits inside it.
Start day one: execute the rep at the designed size, mark it in the app, and note how the completion mark actually felt.

quiz.question: The research-backed median time to habit automaticity is roughly…
Missing a single day of a forming habit…
options: Produced no measurable damage in the tracking data — the danger is the miss becoming a break
Means the habit was wrong for you

quiz.question: Kaushal & Rhodes found new gym-goers formed habits when…
options: They set ambitious performance goals early
Cues and context stayed consistent across frequent early sessions
They varied routines to prevent boredom
They scheduled sessions at different times to test preferences

quiz.question: The 'smallest viable version' condition exists because…
options: You're wiring the showing-up, not the volume — big versions demand the heroic weather you're eliminating

quiz.question: Franklin's speckled axe teaches that…
options: The system's job is the trend, not the perfect page — perfectionism is the crash in disguise

quiz.question: The correct response to missing two days is…
options: Smallest version tomorrow, non-negotiable, plus structural diagnosis of the cue or size

_quiz_key.explanation: The 21-day figure is folklore; the reviewed data medians land around two months.
One miss is noise in the curve; two unaddressed misses start the break.
Consistency of trigger and context, not enthusiasm, predicted habit formation.
Volume expands trivially after the trigger binds; heroic sizes prevent the binding.
Franklin ran the system for years, speckled — and credited it anyway.
Structural diagnosis and repair — you're the architect, not the defendant.

D1.explain_back_prompt: Explain why automaticity beats motivation as a foundation, the five design conditions, and what the honest ~60-day timeline changes about how you read week three.
D1.citation: Singh et al. 2024 (systematic review — median time to automaticity); Kaushal & Rhodes 2015 (gym habit formation); Lally et al. 2010 (66-day median, curve shape, single-miss finding)
D1.confidence_note: STRONG (<6 months): median time-to-automaticity ~59-66 days; don't expect it done at week 3.

D2.big_picture: 'Grit' became a sermon; the data underneath it says something sharper and more useful. When researchers took the construct apart, only one piece carried real predictive weight: perseverance of effort — showing up, finishing, sustaining effort through bad weeks. The 'never change your goal' half measured almost nothing. This module teaches you to track the part that works, deliberately decoupled from goal-rigidity — so you can pivot strategies like an investor while never once failing to show up.

chapters.title: The sermon and the data
chapters.body:
In the mid-2010s, 'grit' became the most famous idea in achievement psychology: passion and perseverance for long-term goals, measured by a short questionnaire, credited with predicting success at West Point, in spelling bees, in life. A bestselling book, one of the most-watched TED talks ever, school districts building curricula around it. The concept had a scientist behind it — Angela Duckworth, a serious researcher whose instrument and honesty are better than the hype that engulfed her construct — and it had the perfect shape for a sermon: success comes to those who want it longer.

Then, in 2017, Marcus Credé, Michael Tynan, and Peter Harms did to grit what this curriculum's Track B taught you to do to every attractive story: they ran the meta-analysis. Pooling hundreds of samples and tens of thousands of participants, three findings restructured the concept. First, grit's overall correlation with performance was real but modest — far weaker than the public narrative implied, and much weaker than boring old predictors like study habits or cognitive ability. Second, grit overlapped so heavily with conscientiousness — a Big Five personality trait psychologists had measured for decades — that it was hard to defend as a new discovery at all; their estimated correlation ran around .84, close enough to identity to suggest rebranding. Third — and this is the finding your entire module hangs on, so take it with its own honest sizing — when they split the questionnaire into its two halves, the halves behaved differently. Perseverance of effort — items about finishing what you begin, working hard, sustaining effort through setbacks — carried most of the predictive weight; consistency of interest — items about never changing goals — predicted almost nothing. Two calibration notes the sermon never includes: this sub-facet split is exactly why the module's tag reads MODERATE rather than STRONG, and even perseverance's edge over plain conscientiousness was itself modest. You are training a well-evidenced old trait under a newer name — not a superpower psychology just discovered.

Sit with what that means, because it's liberating in a precise way. The evidence supports the unglamorous half — show up, finish, push through bad weeks — and quietly discards the romantic half about lifelong unwavering passion for one fixed goal. Which is exactly the shape a founder needs: your A5 module just taught you to change direction on evidence. The data says that changing goals was never the problem. Quitting effort was.
chapters.key_takeaway: Grit's predictive power lives almost entirely in perseverance of effort — the 'never change your goal' half measured nearly nothing.

figure.name: Angela Duckworth
figure.caption: Built the grit construct — then the meta-analysis showed its power lives in one half: perseverance of effort, not fixed passion.

columns.label: Perseverance of effort
points: Carries nearly all the predictive weight

chapters.title: 5,127 prototypes, zero fixed designs
chapters.body:
James Dyson's origin story is usually told as a grit sermon: fifteen years, 5,127 failed prototypes, near-bankruptcy, and then the bagless vacuum cleaner that made him one of Britain's richest men. Every telling emphasizes the count — so note first that the count is Dyson's own oft-repeated figure, never independently audited: hold the digits loosely and the texture firmly. Almost no telling notices what the texture actually contains — and what it contains is this module's distinction, walking around in workshop clothes.

Each of those 5,127 prototypes was a changed design. Dyson wasn't building the same vacuum 5,127 times while believing harder; he was running an iteration loop — build, test, observe the failure, modify one variable, build again — borrowed from his engineering hero's method of empirical trial. The cyclone geometry changed. Materials changed. The separation approach changed, over and over. What never changed was the showing up: every day, back to the shed, next prototype, through years when his wife's salary carried the family and the industry told him the idea was worthless (the established manufacturers declined the invention — because, in Dyson's own telling, their profits lived in replacement bags — a rebuff that fueled his decision to manufacture himself). Effort: perfectly persistent. Ideas: relentlessly disposable. That's the pairing the Credé data endorses, and it's the exact inverse of how the sermon reads the same story.

Now run the counterfactual to sharpen it. A Dyson with fixed-goal 'consistency of interest' at the design level — prototype #1 rebuilt with more willpower 5,127 times — dies in the shed, having demonstrated magnificent useless persistence. A Dyson with iteration but no perseverance of effort quits in year three when the count hits a few hundred and the money gets thin. The invention required the asymmetry: rigid about the sessions, fluid about the solutions.

Write the founder translation on your wall: persevere at the level of effort and process; iterate at the level of strategy and design. Your A5 direction memo can be revised, your product can pivot, your pitch can be rebuilt weekly — while your show-up rate stays at Dyson levels. The two loyalties never conflict, because they were never the same thing.
chapters.key_takeaway: Rigid about sessions, fluid about solutions — Dyson persevered at the effort level while changing the design 5,127 times.

figure.name: James Dyson
figure.caption: 5,127 prototypes — every one a CHANGED design. Rigid about showing up, ruthlessly fluid about the solution.

chapters.title: What to measure: the effort ledger
chapters.body:
If perseverance of effort is the facet that pays, it needs what every asset in this curriculum gets: a ledger. Feelings about your work ethic are unreliable narrators — vivid good days and catastrophized bad ones, survivorship-filtered by memory exactly as B4 warned. The fix is the same as every other module's fix: write the numbers down and let the trend testify.

Three metrics, chosen because they operationalize the actual questionnaire items that carried the predictive weight — and because each one is binary-ish, cheap to log, and hard to argue with.

Metric one: show-up rate. Of the sessions you committed to — study blocks, training, client work sprints, the D1 habit — what fraction actually happened, at any size? Never-zero passes count fully; this is D1's machinery feeding D2's ledger. Target isn't 100% (that's the speckled-axe error); it's a rate that stays high through bad weeks — the whole point of the next chapter.

Metric two: finish rate. Of the things you started — proposals, features, modules in this curriculum, prototypes — what fraction reached a defined done-or-killed state? Note the crucial disjunction: killed counts as finished. A project explicitly killed by an A5-style review is a completed decision; a project abandoned by silent drift is the failure mode this metric exists to catch. 'Finishes what he begins' includes finishing the decision to stop.

Metric three: bad-week delta. The sharpest instrument of the three: your show-up rate in your worst weeks specifically, versus your average. A founder who runs 95% in good weeks and 20% in bad ones doesn't have a discipline system; he has good weather and a costume. The delta — how little your floor drops below your ceiling — is the single number that most honestly measures perseverance of effort as the researchers meant it: sustained effort through setbacks.

Log weekly, review monthly alongside your C4 promise log and B3 journal — they're siblings. The ledger takes five minutes a week and, within a quarter, replaces 'am I disciplined?' — an anxiety — with 'what's my bad-week delta?' — an engineering question.
chapters.key_takeaway: Show-up rate, finish rate (killed counts), and the bad-week delta — three numbers that turn 'am I disciplined?' into engineering.

chapters.title: The bad-week protocol
chapters.body:
Your culminating artifact for this module is specific: a self-review showing consistent effort logged through a bad week. Not a good week performed well — a bad one survived on the record. So this chapter is the protocol for the week that's coming: the client crisis, the flu, the deal that dies, the family thing, the black-mood stretch that has no cause at all. It will arrive within the nine weeks of your D1 build. Good — the artifact needs it.

Rule one: pre-shrink everything, using D1's machinery. Before the bad week (that means now), write the drained-day version of every committed session: the two-minute study rep, the one-line C3 capture, the single forecast check. During the storm, you're not deciding what to cut — the decisions were made in calm weather. Bad weeks are when decision quality is worst; the protocol's whole design principle is: never design during the storm.

Rule two: the floor is showing up, and showing up is redefined. In a crisis week, opening the ledger and logging 'session: minimal version, done' IS the perseverance the research measures. You are not maintaining output — output legitimately falls; clients and health eat hours. You're maintaining the effort infrastructure so there's nothing to rebuild when the weather clears. The crash cycle's real cost was never the missed week; it was the three-week rebuild after.

Rule three: no compensatory heroics. The double session after a miss, the all-nighter to 'catch up' — these feel like discipline and function as its opposite: they turn the ledger into a guilt economy and usually borrow from sleep, which Track E will show you is the one loan with loan-shark interest. The protocol after a bad stretch is boring by design: normal size, next scheduled slot, trend over drama.

Rule four: log the week honestly and write the two-paragraph review after — what shrank, what held, what the delta was. That review IS the artifact. A founder holding receipts that say 'worst week of the quarter: show-up 78%, floor versions, no rebuild needed' owns something no motivational surge ever produced: evidence about himself.
chapters.key_takeaway: Pre-shrink in calm weather, count minimal versions as full wins, ban compensatory heroics, and write the review — the bad week IS the artifact.

diagram.title: When the storm hits
nodes: Log every showing-up as a win

chapters.title: Becoming the one who shows up
chapters.body:
Zoom out to what this module actually installs, because it's subtler than 'work hard' and more valuable.

First, it resolves a tension that quietly torments ambitious people: the fear that flexibility is weakness. You've now got the data-backed license: goal-changing predicted nothing; effort-quitting predicted everything. So when your A5 review kills a direction, when the market moves and Gotham's roadmap bends, when you drop a service line that isn't working — none of that touches your discipline identity, because your identity was never 'the guy with the unchanging plan.' It's 'the guy who shows up regardless of the plan.' That identity survives every pivot, which is precisely what makes pivoting cheap. Founders whose self-worth is welded to a specific goal defend the goal past its expiry date — sunk-cost error with an ego bodyguard. Yours is welded to the ledger.

Second, it converts discipline from a mood into a portfolio of numbers with trends — and numbers with trends respond to engineering. Show-up rate low? Cue problem or size problem; D1 has the tools. Finish rate low? Too many parallel starts; kill or complete before opening new fronts. Bad-week delta ugly? The pre-shrunk versions weren't real; resize them. Every discipline failure now routes to a structural fix instead of a character verdict — the same move C3 made for calls and B3 made for decisions, completing the pattern: this curriculum never puts you on trial; it puts your systems on the bench.

Third — the long game — the ledger compounds into reputation. The C4 module showed that markets price predictability; the effort ledger is where your predictability is manufactured. And know the collapse's face, because it never announces itself: the founder whose ledger rots reschedules twice, delivers at eighty percent, calls it iteration — and six months later wonders why clients hedge every commitment back at him. The founder with receipts for two years of 85%+ show-up through bad weeks never has that conversation; the discipline leaks from everything he ships.

Definition of done, cold: the three-metric ledger live for four weeks; one genuine bad week logged under the protocol with its two-paragraph review written; finish-rate list current with at least one explicit kill counted as a finish; and the decoupling stated in your own words in the explain-back. Then D3 — where you learn to check, quarterly, that any of this is still actually running.
chapters.key_takeaway: Identity welded to the ledger, not the goal — pivots become cheap, discipline becomes engineering, and predictability compounds into reputation.

exercises.task: Set up the effort ledger with the three metrics and backfill last week honestly: show-up rate, finish rate, and your recent worst-week delta.
Write your pre-shrunk bad-week versions for every currently committed session — calm-weather decisions, on paper.
Audit your open starts: list everything begun and unfinished, then mark each for completion or explicit kill this month.
Write three sentences distinguishing a strategy pivot from an effort quit, using one real example from your own last quarter.

quiz.question: Credé, Tynan & Harms' 2017 meta-analysis found grit overall to be…
options: A modest predictor overlapping heavily with conscientiousness

quiz.question: Which grit facet carried nearly all the predictive weight?
Dyson's 5,127 prototypes exemplify…
options: Consistency of interest applied at the design level
Rigid effort persistence combined with relentless design iteration
Perseverance rewarded only after external funding arrived
Iteration succeeding because the goal itself kept changing
quiz.objective: Read the case through the facet split

quiz.question: In the finish-rate metric, a project explicitly killed by review counts as…
options: Finished — the decision reached a defined end-state

quiz.question: The bad-week delta measures…
options: How little your show-up floor drops below your ceiling
Number of bad weeks per quarter

quiz.question: Compensatory heroics (double sessions after misses) are banned because…
options: They turn the ledger into a guilt economy and usually borrow from sleep

_quiz_key.explanation: Real but modest, and largely conscientiousness rebranded — the sermon outran the data.
Show up, finish, push through — the unglamorous half does the work.
Every prototype changed; the showing-up never did.
Silent drift is the failure mode; explicit kills are completed decisions.
Sustained effort through setbacks — the facet, quantified.
Boring resumption beats dramatic repayment; sleep loans carry loan-shark interest.

D2.explain_back_prompt: Explain the grit correction — which facet predicts and which doesn't — and why that decoupling makes strategy pivots cheap while keeping your show-up rate sacred.
D2.citation: Credé, Tynan & Harms 2017 (grit meta-analysis, r≈.84 with conscientiousness); Duckworth (original construct); Dyson (self-reported iteration record)
D2.confidence_note: STRONG (grit ≈ conscientiousness) / MODERATE (sub-facet): only perseverance-of-effort carries real weight.

D3.big_picture: Here's an uncomfortable truth this curriculum tells you straight: the science of habits goes quiet after about a year. Nobody has solid data on which habits survive to year two and which quietly die — so this module is labeled THEORETICAL and built as a hedge: a quarterly durability review that inspects every system you've installed, catches the silent deaths early, and reallocates effort before decay becomes collapse. It's the practice of maintaining infrastructure the research can't yet guarantee.

chapters.title: Where the research goes dark
chapters.body:
Every module in this curriculum has leaned on evidence, and each has told you honestly how strong it is. This one opens with a confession about the whole field: habit science is a science of beginnings. The studies that gave D1 its numbers — the ~66-day median time to automaticity (with individual estimates spanning 18 to 250-plus days), the cue-consistency findings — track people for weeks or months. A handful reach a year. Beyond that, the literature thins to almost nothing: there is very little rigorous data on whether the exercise habit formed in study week ten is still running in real-life month twenty, which habits decay and which persist, or what maintenance actually requires at year two. It's not that researchers found habits die — it's that mostly, nobody has looked. Long-term tracking is expensive, funding cycles are short, and 'do people still do the thing three years later?' is exactly the kind of unglamorous question that goes unfunded.

Meanwhile, everything you actually care about is a multi-year proposition. The A1 study engine matters if it runs for a decade. The effort ledger, the promise log, the review loops — their value is almost entirely in their persistence, and their persistence is precisely what the evidence can't certify. You've been trained by Track B for exactly this situation: a decision that must be made under acknowledged uncertainty. The options: assume the systems will hold (hope, dressed as confidence), rebuild everything at each collapse (the crash cycle at yearly scale), or install a monitoring layer that detects decay early and cheaply — a hedge whose cost is small and whose payoff is avoiding silent, compounding rot.

That's what this module is, and that's why its confidence tag reads THEORETICAL: the quarterly durability review is not a proven mechanism from a trial; it's an engineered response to a known evidence gap, built from adjacent principles that ARE well-supported — feedback loops (C3's foundation), measurement over memory (B-track's foundation), and structural rather than moral diagnosis (D1's foundation). This curriculum flags its bets honestly. This is a bet. It's also, as the next chapters show, one the most effective people in history made instinctively.
chapters.key_takeaway: Habit science tracks beginnings, not year two — the review is an honest hedge against a real evidence gap, built from proven parts.

chapters.title: Drucker's nine-month experiment
chapters.body:
Peter Drucker — the Austrian-American thinker who more or less invented management as a discipline, advising everyone from General Motors to churches across a seventy-year career — ran one personal practice that he credited above all others, and it happens to be the closest thing this module has to a validated ancestor. He called it feedback analysis. Whenever he made a significant decision or committed to a course of action, he wrote down what he expected to happen. Then — this is the part everyone skips — nine to twelve months later, he went back and compared the actual results against the written expectation. Not his memory of the expectation: the paper. He claimed the method traced back centuries through Jesuit and Calvinist practice, and that within two or three years it reliably revealed where a person's real strengths were, where their intellectual arrogance hid, and which habits of behavior were sabotaging results.

Notice the design choices, because your quarterly review inherits all three. First, the long lag: Drucker deliberately reviewed at a horizon where narratives had finished forming — which is exactly where memory is least trustworthy and written records most valuable. B3's monthly review catches decisions; the durability review catches what months of drift did to systems, and drift is invisible at short range by definition. Second, expectations written in advance: the review compares reality against a fixed benchmark, not against the moving target of what you now claim you meant. Your version: each quarter's review ends by writing what you expect each system to look like next quarter — the artifact the next review will grade. Third, the target of the analysis is allocation: Drucker's conclusion from decades of feedback analysis was brutally practical — put your effort where the evidence shows your strengths compound, and stop feeding what the paper proves you're merely mediocre at. Applied to your systems: the review isn't just 'is the habit alive?' but 'is it still earning its slot?' A habit can be perfectly automatic and no longer worth its minutes — that's not durability, that's a squatter. The quarterly review evicts squatters and reinvests the time.
chapters.key_takeaway: Write expectations in advance, review at long lag against the paper, and reallocate toward what compounds — Drucker's loop is the review's ancestor.

figure.name: Peter Drucker
figure.caption: Feedback analysis: write the expectation now, grade it against reality nine months later — paper versus narrative, for fifty years.

chapters.title: The emperor's maintenance log
chapters.body:
Around 170 AD, the most powerful man in the Western world sat in a military camp on the Danube frontier, at war, surrounded by plague and court intrigue, and wrote notes to himself about not becoming what power makes of people. Marcus Aurelius never intended the Meditations for publication — the working title history assigned it was closer to 'To Himself' — and that's precisely what makes it this chapter's exhibit. It isn't philosophy performed for an audience; it's the private maintenance log of a man running recurring inspection on his own operating system, for years, under maximum load.

Read it with an engineer's eye and the structure jumps out. The same principles recur dozens of times — on anger, on mortality, on doing the duty in front of him, on not being corrupted by praise — not because he forgot writing them, but because he understood something this module's evidence gap makes formal: internal systems decay under load and require re-installation. 'You will drift unless you return to these,' he effectively tells himself, then returns, book after book. He inspects his responses to specific recent events — a courtier's flattery, a betrayal, his own irritation — the way your C3 practice inspects calls: incident, principle, adjustment. And he does it while emperor: the busiest, most consequential schedule in his hemisphere included recurring time to check whether the person running the empire was still running to spec. The practice survived him into the tradition of the examen and journal-review that Drucker later cited — a two-thousand-year lineage of exactly one idea: what you don't inspect, you lose slowly and silently.

The founder translation is almost too direct. Your systems now number a dozen — study engine, ledgers, journals, reviews, rooms, training. Under load (and your load will only grow), they won't fail loudly; they'll drift: the journal entries getting thinner, the AAR becoming a checkbox, the 'protected' sleep window quietly eroding to six hours. Marcus's insight is that the drift is universal — it happened to a Stoic emperor with the best philosophical training of his age — and therefore the countermeasure must be scheduled, not summoned by noticing. You will not notice. That's what drift means. The calendar notices for you.
chapters.key_takeaway: Systems decay silently under load — even an emperor needed scheduled re-inspection. You won't notice drift; the calendar must.

figure.name: Marcus Aurelius
figure.caption: The Meditations are a private maintenance log — the same principles re-installed again and again, because drift spares no one, not even emperors.

chapters.title: The review protocol
chapters.body:
The instrument itself: ninety minutes, once a quarter, on the calendar as a standing appointment (this app can hold the slot). Four passes over everything you've installed, ending with Drucker's written expectations for next quarter.

Pass one — the census. List every system this curriculum has installed that you claim is running: D1 habit(s), effort ledger, forecast log, decision journal, base-rate procedure, post-call captures, promise log, relationship files, monthly letters, recurring room, training and sleep protocols. For each: when did it last actually run? The census alone catches the biggest lie — systems you believe are alive because they were alive in March. Check the receipts, not the memory: this app's own session, streak, and ledger records, plus your documents' edit dates.

Pass two — the triage. Sort the census three ways. Automatic: running without friction — leave them alone entirely (inspection can perturb what's working; don't fix the unbroken). Decaying: running but thinning — entries shorter, gaps widening, the theater C5 warned about. These get structural diagnosis, D1-style: did the cue break? Did the load grow? Did the size creep up past sustainability? One fix per decaying system, written. Dead: not running for 3+ weeks. For each corpse, the honest fork — revive at smallest viable size with a repaired cue, or bury it deliberately: an explicit kill (D2 taught you kills count as finishes) that frees its slot without shame-debt.

Pass three — the squatter audit, Drucker's allocation question. Of the automatic systems, does each still earn its minutes against what those minutes could buy now? Your quarter's context changed — A5 may have redirected the study engine's target, a client roster change may have altered which relationships need files. Durable is necessary, not sufficient; re-point what persists.

Pass four — the expectations memo. One paragraph: what each surviving system should look like at next quarter's review — frequencies, sizes, outputs. Date it. Next quarter grades against this paper, not against your improved recollection. That memo is what makes this a Drucker loop instead of a quarterly mood.
chapters.key_takeaway: Census from receipts, triage into automatic/decaying/dead, evict the squatters, write next quarter's expectations — ninety minutes, four passes.

diagram.title: Ninety minutes, quarterly
nodes: Squatter audit: still earning its slot?

chapters.title: Holding uncertainty like a professional
chapters.body:
This module closes Track D's systems-building sequence (one lesson remains in the track: D-LIT, a health-literacy module about burnout and depression rather than a systems one), so step back and see what the track built, and what this last piece adds that the others couldn't.

D1 gave you construction: the five conditions that wire a behavior into infrastructure. D2 gave you the load-bearing metric: perseverance of effort, tracked through bad weeks, decoupled from goal-rigidity. D3 adds the thing every real infrastructure system has and almost no personal-development system includes: scheduled inspection with an honest maintenance budget. Roads get inspected. Aircraft get inspected on intervals measured in flight-hours, not in whether anyone noticed a problem. The insight of maintenance engineering — inherited here through Drucker and a Stoic emperor — is that inspection intervals must be set by policy precisely because failure onset is silent. Your systems are now infrastructure; treat them with infrastructure's discipline.

But the deeper lesson of this module is the one flagged in its confidence tag, and it generalizes far beyond habits. You will spend your career making commitments in territory where the research goes dark — markets nobody has data on, technologies too new for base rates, decade-scale bets no study will ever de-risk. The intellectually honest response is neither paralysis nor bravado; it's exactly what this module modeled: name the gap out loud, build the hedge from adjacent evidence, size its cost to the uncertainty (ninety minutes a quarter is a rounding error), and instrument it so reality reports back on schedule. That pattern — bet, flag, hedge, instrument — is how a Track B graduate operates everywhere the map runs out. This module is a habit lesson on the surface and a masterclass in professional uncertainty underneath.

Definition of done, cold: the first full review executed with all four passes; at least one system honestly triaged as decaying or dead, with its fix or burial documented; the squatter audit run with one reallocation considered; and next quarter's expectations memo written, dated, and stored where the next review will find it. The review itself then recurs — which means this module is never finished, only maintained. That's the point.
chapters.key_takeaway: Bet, flag, hedge, instrument — the review is maintenance engineering for your systems, and a template for every decision the research can't de-risk.

exercises.task: Run the census-and-triage in one pass: list every installed system with its last-run date from receipts, then mark each automatic, decaying, or dead — with the one structural fix or deliberate burial for each non-automatic one.
Write your first expectations memo: one dated paragraph on what each surviving system should look like in 90 days.
Put the quarterly review on the calendar as a recurring 90-minute appointment, and set where the memo will live.
Write the review's own failsafe: one sentence naming what re-triggers the review if a quarter gets skipped (a person who asks, a recurring reminder with an alarm) — and install that trigger now.

Noticing is unreliable only for beginners

_quiz_key.explanation: The field studies beginnings; year two is dark — the module says so and hedges.
Fixed benchmarks beat moving narratives — reality grades the paper.
Written to himself, repeating principles because drift spares no one.

D3.explain_back_prompt: Explain why this module is honestly THEORETICAL, and walk through the four passes of your quarterly review — including what happens to decaying, dead, and squatting systems.
D3.citation: Gap in habit-durability literature (named honestly); Drucker, 'Managing Oneself' (feedback analysis); Marcus Aurelius, Meditations (recurring self-inspection)
D3.confidence_note: THEORETICAL: almost no research tracks habit survival past 12-18 months; this review is a hedge.

D-LIT.big_picture: Founders run elevated mental-health risk — the data is imperfect but the direction is credible — and most can't tell the difference between the two conditions they're most likely to meet. Burnout responds to rest; depression persists after the workload drops. Confusing them costs months at best. This module builds real literacy: what each one is, the test that distinguishes them, and the three protective levers with the best evidence — plus a project that turns literacy into your personal early-warning protocol, built now, while you're well.

chapters.title: The skill nobody puts on the roadmap
chapters.body:
Every module in this curriculum assumes a functioning founder. The study engine, the forecast practice, the recurring rooms — all of it runs on the machine between your ears, and this track has spent three modules building discipline systems that put sustained load on that machine. So here is the module the hustle culture around you will never assign, framed the only way this curriculum knows how: as engineering. High-performance systems under sustained load require failure-mode literacy. You know what overtraining looks like in boxing — you taper, you deload, you don't call it weakness. This is the cognitive equivalent, and at eighteen, building two companies, you're taking the load early. That's an advantage for compounding skill and a reason to build the safety systems early too.

The stakes are not hypothetical, and the evidence — held with Track B honesty — says your profession concentrates them. Michael Freeman and colleagues' 2019 study compared entrepreneurs to matched non-entrepreneurs and found founders reported significantly higher lifetime rates of mental-health conditions — the paper's depression figures ran around thirty percent of entrepreneurs versus roughly half that in the comparison group, with elevated ADHD and substance-use rates too. Hold the caveats firmly: self-report samples, possible selection effects (people who join founder studies may differ), and cross-sectional design meaning causality is unproven — maybe founding strains minds, maybe restless minds found companies, likely both. The percentages are soft. The direction — that this job correlates with elevated psychiatric load — is credible enough that ignoring it fails your own B5 standard: it would be an unpriced, unsurvivable-if-wrong bet.

And the specific failure this module prevents is misdiagnosis of yourself. The two conditions a founder most plausibly meets — burnout and depression — feel superficially similar from inside: exhaustion, flatness, work turning to sludge. But they are different phenomena with different mechanics and, critically, different fixes. Treat depression like burnout and you'll take a vacation that changes nothing and conclude you're broken. Treat burnout like a character flaw and you'll push harder into the exact fire that's consuming you. Literacy is the difference between months lost and weeks managed.
chapters.key_takeaway: Founders carry elevated, imperfectly-measured mental-health risk — and misreading burnout as depression (or either as weakness) costs months.

diagram.title: Why this is a business module

chapters.title: Burnout: the occupational fire
chapters.body:
The word 'burnout' has a birthplace: a free clinic in 1970s New York. Herbert Freudenberger, a psychoanalyst who had survived Nazi Germany as a child and worked punishing double days — private practice plus volunteer nights at the clinic — began noticing a pattern in the clinic's most dedicated staff, and in himself: idealistic, driven people gradually becoming exhausted, cynical, and ineffective, specifically in relation to the work they had loved. He borrowed the term from drug culture ('burned out') and published the first clinical description in 1974. The detail that matters most for your literacy: he observed it in the most committed people. Burnout is not a slacker's condition — it selects for exactly your profile, because you cannot burn what was never lit.

The modern, evidence-based definition crystallized through decades of research (most influentially Christina Maslach's) into three dimensions, and in 2019 the World Health Organization wrote it into the ICD-11 with a classification that is itself the lesson: burnout is an occupational phenomenon, not a medical illness — defined as resulting from chronic workplace stress that has not been successfully managed. Dimension one: exhaustion — energy depletion that sleep stops fixing. Dimension two: cynicism and mental distance — the work you cared about turns to irony; clients become annoyances; you catch yourself sneering at the mission. Dimension three: reduced professional efficacy — the felt sense that you're getting worse at the job, often preceding any measurable decline.

The structural insight packed into that WHO classification: burnout is about the relationship between a person and a workload, which is why its remedies are relational — rest that's actually rest, workload redesign, restored control over one's time, recovery of meaning. And it's why the distinguishing feature you'll use in chapter four is this: burnout tracks the work. Distance from the stressor — a real break, a redesigned role — produces genuine improvement. The fire is in the job. Move away from the job, and you cool.
chapters.key_takeaway: Burnout is occupational — exhaustion, cynicism, felt incompetence, caused by unmanaged work stress — and it genuinely improves with distance from the stressor.

figure.name: Herbert Freudenberger
figure.caption: Coined 'burnout' in 1974 after watching it consume the clinic's MOST dedicated staff — and himself. You cannot burn what was never lit.

chapters.title: Depression: the fire that follows you home
chapters.body:
Depression is a different animal, and the difference begins with jurisdiction. Burnout lives in the ICD as an occupational phenomenon; major depressive disorder is a medical condition — a mood disorder with diagnostic criteria, established treatments, and no requirement of any workplace cause. Its signature isn't tiredness; it's pervasiveness. Low mood or a loss of interest and pleasure (anhedonia — the technical name for food, music, training, wins all going flat) most of the day, nearly every day, for weeks, across domains. Sleep and appetite disturbed in either direction. Concentration degraded. Feelings of worthlessness or inappropriate guilt that attach themselves to anything available. And in serious cases, thoughts of death or self-harm — which are a see-a-professional-now signal, full stop, no protocol or module required.

Here is the distinguishing mechanic, stated as plainly as the science allows: depression persists when the workload drops. The vacation happens and the flatness comes along. The deal closes, the win registers as nothing. Whatever role work stress played in triggering it, the condition has its own engine now, and removing the stressor no longer stops the machine. That's why the misdiagnosis in chapter one is so costly in this direction: a founder treating depression as burnout keeps prescribing himself rest, watches rest fail, and draws the catastrophic conclusion — 'even rest doesn't work, so it must be me.' It was never a workload problem. It's a treatable medical condition being managed with the wrong tool.

One more piece of literacy, historical and deliberate: Abraham Lincoln lived with documented, recurrent, severe depression — his contemporaries called it melancholy; his episodes were witnessed and recorded by friends who feared for him, a record assembled in Joshua Wolf Shenk's scholarly study Lincoln's Melancholy — and he prosecuted the most demanding presidency in American history while carrying it. The point is not that suffering is required for greatness. It's narrower and more useful: depression is compatible with capability and worth, it afflicts the strongest people on record, and managing it — with help — is what serious people do. The founder mythology that treats psychiatric illness as disqualifying is not just cruel; it is historically illiterate.
chapters.key_takeaway: Depression is medical, pervasive, and persists after the workload drops — and it is fully compatible with capability, as the strongest people on record demonstrate.

figure.name: Abraham Lincoln
figure.caption: Lived with documented recurrent depression — witnessed by friends, named openly in his era — while carrying the most demanding presidency in American history.

chapters.title: The rest test and the honest data
chapters.body:
Now assemble the two chapters into the diagnostic literacy this module exists to deliver — with the strong caveat stated first and meant: this is literacy for self-monitoring and earlier help-seeking, not self-diagnosis. The differential between burnout, depression, anxiety disorders, thyroid dysfunction (an underactive thyroid can mimic depression's fatigue and flatness — one reason clinicians often run blood work first), and half a dozen other conditions belongs to clinicians. Your job is to notice earlier and describe better — which, when you do sit in front of a professional, makes their job dramatically faster.

The core instrument is the rest test, and you now understand its mechanics from both sides. Question: when you get genuine distance from work — a real weekend, a true break, the E1 sleep window held for two weeks — does the system recover? Substantial recovery points toward burnout: the occupational relationship is the fire, and workload redesign is the lever. No recovery — flatness that follows you into the break, anhedonia that makes the rest itself joyless — points toward something with its own engine, and toward professional evaluation. Secondary markers sharpen the read: burnout's emotional signature is cynicism aimed at the work specifically, while depression's is worthlessness aimed at yourself globally; burnout typically wants the old self back ('I used to love this'), while depression often can't remember wanting anything.

Apply the same honesty to the founder data you're carrying from chapter one. Freeman's percentages — the doubled depression rate — are soft numbers from self-report, and this module refuses to weaponize them into either doom or dismissal. The Track B reading: direction credible, magnitude uncertain, and the expected-value calculation unchanged by the uncertainty — because the protective levers in the next chapter are cheap, evidence-backed for general mental health regardless of the founder-specific numbers, and carry essentially no downside. You don't need the exact probability of rain to justify owning an umbrella this inexpensive.

One rule to install verbatim: any thought of self-harm, any 'they'd be better off without me' arithmetic — that is not a data point for your protocol; that is an immediate conversation with a professional or a crisis line. Literacy includes knowing where literacy ends.
chapters.key_takeaway: The rest test: real distance from work → recovery suggests burnout; persistence suggests medical evaluation. Notice earlier, describe better — diagnose never.

chapters.title: The three levers
chapters.body:
Protection now — three levers, ranked by evidence quality, all of them things you build while healthy because none of them can be built quickly while sick.

Lever one: sleep, the non-negotiable. The evidence connecting sleep disruption to mood disorders runs both directions — meta-analytic work (Baglioni and colleagues' synthesis of longitudinal studies) finds insomnia roughly doubles the risk of later depression, and protecting sleep measurably supports mood and cognitive regulation. Track E's first module builds the full protocol; this module adds only the mental-health framing: your protected sleep window is not a productivity tool that can be traded away in crunch weeks. It is the load-bearing wall of the machine everything else runs on, and — D2's rule already said it — the loan against it carries loan-shark interest. Founders overwhelmingly breach sleep first when load rises. Pre-commit, in writing, that it breaches last.

Lever two: real social connection — emphasis on real. The epidemiological evidence linking genuine social ties to mental-health resilience is among the most robust in the field, and founder life erodes exactly this asset: friends become network, conversations become pipeline, and the density of people who know you — not IronLogic's founder, you — quietly drops toward one or zero. The lever is specific: identified humans with whom the mask is off, contact maintained on a cadence that survives busy quarters (your C4 systems know how to do this — point them inward). The isolation crash usually announces itself in retrospect as 'I realized I hadn't had a non-work conversation in two months.' Instrument that number.

Lever three: early help-seeking, reframed as the professional move. The single largest modifiable factor in outcomes for both conditions is lag — the months between onset and action. The mythology says founders tough it out; the actual elite pattern, from athletes with performance psychologists to executives with coaches and therapists, is professionalized support engaged early and without drama. Know now, while well, what your access path is: what therapy costs in Kingston, what's covered, who answers crisis lines (in Jamaica and regionally), which doctor you'd call first. Researching that path takes one evening while healthy and can be nearly impossible from inside a depressive episode. That asymmetry is the entire argument for the project below.
chapters.key_takeaway: Sleep breaches last, real connection gets instrumented, and the help path gets researched now — every lever is cheap while well and expensive while sick.

diagram.title: Build order, while healthy
nodes: Research the help path this week

chapters.title: Literacy into protocol
chapters.body:
The culminating move of this module — and of Track D — is converting everything above into a written instrument: your personal early-warning protocol, the project attached to this checkpoint. Here is why it must be written, and written now. Every skill this module taught degrades precisely when it's needed: the conditions it monitors for attack the monitoring — depression distorts self-assessment toward 'this is just who I am,' burnout's cynicism dismisses the whole framework as another wellness performance. The protocol is a letter from your well self to your struggling self, written by the version of you with working judgment, so that the version without it doesn't have to improvise. It's the same principle as B5's pre-committed kill criteria and D1's calm-weather lapse playbook: never design during the storm.

What the protocol contains — the project brief formalizes it: your personal baseline, described concretely (what your normal energy, sleep, training, social contact, and work pleasure actually look like — specific enough that deviation is detectable); your early-warning markers, drawn from this module's dimensions but personalized (the leading indicators that historically precede your bad stretches — the training sessions that stop, the messages that go unanswered); the rest-test procedure, pre-designed (what 'genuine distance' means for you, for how long, and what recovery versus persistence would look like); the escalation ladder — at which markers you tell a named person, at which you contact a professional, at which it's a crisis line, with the actual names and numbers filled in; and the standing review — the protocol gets thirty seconds in every D3 quarterly, so it stays current as your life changes.

Two integrity rules for building it. Honesty over aesthetics: the markers must be your real historical tells, including any this module's tidy categories don't cover. And a witness: at least one named person — the mask-off kind from lever two — should know the protocol exists and what their role in it is. A tripwire nobody can hear doesn't protect anyone.

Definition of done is the project's rubric. The becoming, said once and plainly: the founder who treats his mind as infrastructure — inspected, instrumented, professionally maintained — is not the soft one in the room. He's the one still in the room in year ten.
chapters.key_takeaway: The protocol is a letter from your well self to your struggling self — baseline, markers, rest test, escalation ladder with real names — written now, witnessed, reviewed quarterly.

exercises.task: Write your concrete baseline: normal energy, sleep, training cadence, social contact, and what work pleasure actually feels like for you.
List your five personal early-warning markers from past bad stretches — the real tells, not generic symptoms.
Start your help path: find two Kingston-area therapy options with costs and Jamaica's crisis line number, written down — the full path gets finished in the project.
Count your mask-off humans and last contact dates; schedule one non-work conversation this week.

A letter from working judgment to compromised judgment — B5's pre-commitment logic.

project_brief.title: Your early-warning protocol
project_brief.description: Write the complete personal early-warning document: baseline, personalized markers, the rest-test procedure, and an escalation ladder with real names and numbers — then give it a witness and wire it into the quarterly review. Built now, while well, because none of it can be built well while sick. This is Track D's capstone: the discipline systems' safety system.
